commonProblems.h2: Common Renault Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: Renault engines deliver strong value but face well-documented reliability challenges, particularly the 1.2 TCe petrol (timing chain stretch) and 1.5 dCi diesel (injector/EGR failures) families that dominate replacement demand across the UK parc. Every rebuilt engine supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded internal components designed to address the original failure point — plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. The problem groups below act as a decision tool: compare realistic repair tiers, understand when replacement offers better long-term value, and get instant quotes from trusted UK Renault specialists.

problems.h4: Timing Chain Failure – Why Renault 1.2 TCe Engines Fail Prematurely
problems.affectedModels: Renault Clio IV, Captur, Megane IV, Scenic III (2012–2018, H5F/H5Ft 1.2 TCe engine codes)
problems.rootCause: The wet-belt timing chain tensioner loses hydraulic pressure over time, allowing chain stretch that disrupts valve timing; combined with oil dilution from direct injection, this accelerates wear on chain guides and sprockets.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace hydraulic tensioner, inspect chain wear, reset engine management; only viable if caught before chain stretch causes valve contact | May extend life 12–24 months if no secondary damage; not suitable once rattling noise or misfire codes appear

| Engine-out replacement of chain, guides, tensioner, sprockets; includes oil pump inspection and updated tensioner design | Realistic 40,000–60,000 mile solution; cost approaches replacement value on higher-mileage vehicles

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt / Reconditioned H5F Engine
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of bench-tested reconditioned engine with upgraded tensioner, reinforced chain guides, revised oil pump; includes 12-month warranty | Restores like-new reliability; most cost-effective route once valve contact or seizure has occurred

problems.recommendation: On a Renault Captur or Megane worth £3,000–£6,000, a full rebuilt H5F engine at £1,950–£3,200 via EngineMarket specialists offers far better value than a £2,800 dealer timing overhaul that may still leave you with oil consumption issues. If you catch early rattling before misfires appear, a specialist tensioner replacement (£450–£750) can buy time — but monitor oil levels weekly.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt 1.2 TCe H5F engine prices for your Clio, Captur or Megane

problems.h4: Injector & EGR Failure – The 1.5 dCi K9K Critical Weakness
problems.affectedModels: Renault Clio III/IV, Megane III/IV, Scenic III, Kadjar, Trafic (2006–2020, K9K 1.5 dCi engine codes)
problems.rootCause: Piezoelectric injectors develop internal leaks under high-pressure cycling, while EGR valves carbon-up from recirculated soot; together they cause poor combustion, DPF clogging, and eventual turbo stress.

repairOptions.tier: | Minor – Single Injector Replacement + EGR Clean
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace one faulty injector, clean EGR valve and cooler, reset adaptation values; diagnostic coding required | Suitable for isolated injector faults before DPF damage; does not address systemic injector wear across all cylinders

repairOptions.tier: | Intermediate – Full Injector Set + EGR/DPF Service
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace all four injectors with updated piezoelectric units, clean or replace EGR assembly, forced DPF regeneration; includes labour for calibration | Restores performance for 30,000–50,000 miles; cost-effective only if turbo and crank sensors are healthy

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt / Reconditioned K9K Engine
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of reconditioned K9K with reinforced injectors, updated EGR cooler, upgraded turbo oil feed; bench-tested with 12-month warranty | Best long-term solution when multiple systems have failed; avoids cascading repairs on high-mileage diesel Renaults

problems.recommendation: For a Renault Megane 1.5 dCi valued at £2,500–£4,500, replacing all four injectors plus EGR service (£950–£1,800 specialist) makes sense only if the turbo and DPF are confirmed healthy. If fault codes span multiple systems or mileage exceeds 100,000, a rebuilt K9K engine at £1,800–£3,500 delivers better reliability and protects your investment.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt K9K 1.5 dCi engines with uprated injectors for your Renault

problems.h4: Turbocharger & Injector Failure – 2.0 dCi M9R Performance Diesel Weakness
problems.affectedModels: Renault Laguna III, Espace IV/V, Koleos, Trafic, Master (2008–2020, M9R 2.0 dCi engine codes)
problems.rootCause: Variable-geometry turbo vanes stick from carbon buildup, causing over-boost and injector hammer; combined with high-pressure fuel pump wear, this leads to catastrophic injector tip failure and piston damage.

repairOptions.tier: | Minor – Turbo Actuator Repair + Injector Coding
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace turbo actuator motor, clean VGT vanes, recode injectors; diagnostic reset of boost control | Temporary fix for early sticking; not viable once turbo shaft play or injector physical damage is confirmed

| Fit remanufactured turbo with updated VGT mechanism, replace all injectors with calibrated units, flush fuel system | Solid 40,000–60,000 mile solution; recommended only if crankshaft bearings and oil pump show no wear

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt / Reconditioned M9R Engine
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of reconditioned M9R with reinforced turbo oil feed, upgraded injectors, revised high-pressure pump; bench-tested with 12-month warranty | Most reliable route for commercial users or high-mileage Espace/Laguna; avoids repeat failures from residual wear

problems.recommendation: On a Renault Trafic or Laguna 2.0 dCi worth £4,000–£8,000, a specialist turbo + injector package (£1,200–£2,100) is viable if the engine has full service history and no knocking noises. For vehicles with intermittent misfires, oil consumption, or over 120,000 miles, a rebuilt M9R engine at £2,200–£3,800 offers peace of mind and protects against costly repeat repairs.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt M9R 2.0 dCi engine prices for your Laguna, Espace or Trafic

problems.h4: Excessive Oil Consumption & Engine Seizure – The Silent Renault Killer
problems.affectedModels: Renault Clio, Megane, Captur, Scenic (2010–2019, H5F 1.2 TCe; K4M 1.6 16V; F4R 2.0 16V petrol engines)
problems.rootCause: Piston ring land wear combined with PCV system clogging allows oil to enter combustion chambers; unchecked consumption leads to oil starvation, bearing wear, and eventual seizure — often with minimal warning.

repairOptions.tier: | Minor – PCV Valve + Oil System Flush
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace PCV valve, perform chemical flush of oil galleries, refill with high-zinc oil; monitor consumption over 1,000 miles | Only suitable for very early-stage consumption (<0.5L/1,000 miles); does not reverse ring land wear

| Engine-out replacement of pistons, rings, and bearings; includes cylinder honing, updated PCV design, and revised oil pump | Viable for low-mileage engines with otherwise healthy heads; cost approaches replacement on older vehicles

| Supply & fit of complete reconditioned engine with upgraded piston rings, revised PCV system, bench-tested; includes 12-month warranty | Most economical solution once seizure or bearing knock is confirmed; restores full reliability with warranty protection

problems.recommendation: If your Renault is consuming more than 0.5L of oil per 1,000 miles or shows blue exhaust smoke, a minor PCV fix won't resolve the underlying ring wear. For engines with knocking noises or low compression, skip intermediate repairs — a rebuilt engine at £1,600–£2,900 via EngineMarket specialists is the only route that delivers lasting value and warranty coverage.
problems.cta: → Get instant quotes for rebuilt petrol Renault engines with upgraded rings and PCV systems

problems.h4: DPF & EGR System Failure – Diesel Renault's Emissions Trap
problems.affectedModels: Renault Megane, Scenic, Kadjar, Trafic, Master (2010–2022, K9K 1.5 dCi; M9R 2.0 dCi; R9M 1.6 dCi diesel engines)
problems.rootCause: Short-trip driving prevents DPF regeneration; soot accumulation combines with EGR carbon to restrict airflow, causing turbo over-speed, injector coking, and eventual engine management shutdown.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Off-vehicle DPF chemical clean, EGR valve/cooler cleaning, forced regeneration cycle; diagnostic reset | Suitable for early warning lights with no power loss; requires immediate change to longer journeys to prevent recurrence

repairOptions.tier: | Intermediate – DPF Replacement + EGR Delete (Where Legal)
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Fit new or refurbished DPF, clean or blank EGR (where emissions-compliant), update ECU mapping; includes road test | Viable for vehicles used primarily off-public roads or in non-emissions-tested regions; check local legality first

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt / Reconditioned Diesel Engine
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of reconditioned diesel engine with updated EGR cooler, reinforced turbo oil feed, bench-tested; includes 12-month warranty | Best solution when DPF failure has caused turbo or injector damage; avoids cascading emissions-system repairs

problems.recommendation: For urban-used Renault diesels showing repeated DPF warnings, a specialist clean (£280–£550) can reset the system — but only if you can commit to regular motorway runs. If power loss, limp mode, or injector codes accompany the DPF light, the damage is likely already done; a rebuilt engine at £1,900–£3,600 protects against repeat failures and includes warranty coverage main dealers won't match.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt diesel Renault engine prices with upgraded EGR/DPF compatibility

types.title: Renault Rebuilt Engines
types.description: A comprehensive overhaul featuring upgraded components to fix known Renault weaknesses, including reinforced timing chain kits for 1.2 TCe units and uprated bearing shells for 1.5 dCi diesel blocks.

types.title: Used Renault Engines
types.description: A low‑mileage, working engine removed from a donor vehicle, pressure tested and verified for compression before dispatch. The most budget‑friendly route when immediate replacement is required.

types.title: Renault Remanufactured Engines
types.description: An as‑new unit rebuilt to strict factory tolerances using only genuine or certified‑equivalent parts, with every component machined, measured and upgraded to original specifications.

types.title: Renault Refurbished Engines
types.description: A thoroughly cleaned, inspected and lightly serviced engine where critical wear items are replaced but the block isn’t fully stripped. A pragmatic, quick‑turnaround solution for older Renaults on a tight budget.

engineSizes.h2: Renault Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model
engineSizes.intro: Every Renault engine size explained — with the models and engine codes that used each displacement — so you can quickly identify the correct replacement unit for your vehicle.

items.title: Renault 1.5L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.5L dCi is Renault's most widely fitted diesel unit, powering millions of UK vehicles from the Clio to the Trafic van. Primarily coded K9K, this engine family spans 2001–present and is known for strong fuel economy but recurring injector, EGR, and DPF-related failures that often drive replacement demand on higher-mileage examples
items.cta: Get quotes for Renault 1.5L diesel engines

items.title: Renault 1.6L Diesel Engines
items.description: Renault's 1.6L dCi family (M5M and R9M codes) replaced many 1.5L applications from 2013 onward, offering improved torque for the Kadjar, Megane, and commercial vans. While more robust than earlier diesels, these units still suffer from EGR cooler failures and turbo actuator sticking, particularly on vehicles used predominantly for urban driving
items.cta: Get quotes for Renault 1.6L diesel engines

items.title: Renault 1.9L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.9L dCi (F9Q) was Renault's workhorse diesel from the late 1990s through the 2010s, fitted to the Laguna, Megane, Scenic, and Trafic. Though generally durable, high-mileage examples commonly experience injector tip failure, turbo wear, and crankshaft position sensor faults that trigger replacement searches
items.cta: Get quotes for Renault 1.9L diesel engines

items.title: Renault 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: Renault's 2.0L dCi (M9R) powers larger models like the Laguna, Espace, Koleos, and commercial vans, offering strong performance but complex emissions hardware. The most frequent catastrophic failure involves injector hammer damaging pistons, combined with VGT turbo sticking from carbon buildup — issues that often make rebuild or replacement more economical than repair
items.cta: Get quotes for Renault 2.0L diesel engines
commonFailurePoints: Injector tip failure causing piston damage

items.title: Renault 2.3L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.3L dCi (M9T) is Renault-Nissan's modern heavy-duty diesel, primarily fitted to the Master and Trafic vans from 2010 onward. Designed for commercial use, it offers strong torque but can suffer from turbocharger wastegate failure and injector coking under high-load conditions, driving replacement demand in the van sector
items.cta: Get quotes for Renault 2.3L diesel engines

items.title: Renault 2.5L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.5L dCi (G9U) served Renault's larger vans and the older Avantime/Espace, offering high torque for towing and commercial use. While robust, these engines commonly experience injector failure and turbocharger wear on high-mileage examples, with replacement often preferred over complex in-situ repairs
items.cta: Get quotes for Renault 2.5L diesel engines

items.title: Renault 3.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: Renault's 3.0L V6 dCi (V9X) was a premium diesel option for the Laguna and Espace, co-developed with Nissan. Complex twin-turbo and emissions hardware make these engines expensive to repair; injector failure, turbo actuator faults, and timing chain tensioner wear are the most common catalysts for replacement searches
items.cta: Get quotes for Renault 3.0L diesel engines

items.title: Renault 0.9L Petrol Engines
items.description: Renault's small-capacity 0.9L TCe (H4B/H4J) turbo petrol engine was introduced to improve fuel economy in the Clio, Captur, and Twingo. While efficient, early units can suffer from turbocharger wastegate sticking and timing chain tensioner issues on the related HRA1 variant, prompting replacement enquiries on higher-mileage examples
items.cta: Compare Renault 0.9L petrol engine prices

items.title: Renault 1.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.2L petrol family spans two distinct eras: the older naturally aspirated D4F 16V (reliable but prone to coil pack failure) and the controversial 2012–2018 H5F/H5Ft TCe turbo unit, which suffers widespread timing chain stretch and oil consumption issues that frequently lead to catastrophic seizure and replacement demand
items.cta: Compare Renault 1.2L petrol engine prices

items.title: Renault 1.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: Renault's 1.4L 16V petrol (K4J) powered entry-level Clio and Megane models through the 2000s. Generally reliable with proper maintenance, common failure points include ignition coil pack failure, timing belt service neglect, and oil consumption from worn valve stem seals on higher-mileage examples
items.cta: Compare Renault 1.4L petrol engine prices

items.title: Renault 1.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.6L petrol family includes the robust K4M 16V and older K7M 8V units, fitted across Clio, Megane, and Scenic models for over two decades. While generally dependable, common issues include coil pack failure, timing belt service intervals missed, and oil consumption from piston ring wear on high-mileage examples
items.cta: Compare Renault 1.6L petrol engine prices
commonFailurePoints: piston ring wear causing oil consumption

items.title: Renault 1.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: Renault's 1.8L petrol engines (F3P 8V and F4R-derived variants) served older Laguna and Megane models. These units are generally robust but can suffer from head gasket failure on overheated examples, oil leaks from valve cover gaskets, and timing belt service neglect driving replacement demand on neglected vehicles
items.cta: Compare Renault 1.8L petrol engine prices

items.title: Renault 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: Renault's 2.0L petrol family includes the naturally aspirated F4R 16V (found in Megane and Laguna) and the turbocharged F4RT TCe performance variant. Common issues include ignition coil failure on NA units, while TCe variants can suffer from turbocharger wastegate sticking and high-pressure fuel pump wear on high-mileage examples
items.cta: Compare Renault 2.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Renault 3.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: Renault's 3.0L V6 petrol (L7X) was a smooth, refined unit fitted to the Laguna and Espace in the late 1990s and early 2000s. While generally reliable with proper maintenance, these engines can suffer from coil pack failure, oil leaks from camshaft seals, and timing belt service complexity that drives replacement over repair on older examples
items.cta: Compare Renault 3.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Renault 1.6L Hybrid Engines
items.description: Renault's E-TECH hybrid system pairs a 1.6L petrol engine (H4M code) with two electric motors and a multi-mode gearbox, fitted to the Clio, Captur, Arkana, and Megane from 2020 onward. Replacement demand remains low as the technology is relatively new, but failures typically involve the high-voltage battery pack or electric motor inverter rather than the petrol engine itself. System / motor code(s): H4M petrol + E-TECH electric motor/generator units
items.cta: Enquire about Renault 1.6L hybrid engine availability

items.title: Renault Electric Motors
items.description: Renault's Zoe and Megane E-Tech Electric use dedicated permanent-magnet synchronous motors rather than traditional engines. Motor failure is rare, but when issues occur they typically involve the inverter module or reduction gearbox; replacement is highly specialist and usually requires OEM-supplied units with calibrated software. System / motor code(s): R135 (Zoe), EEM3 (Megane E-Tech)
items.cta: Enquire about Renault electric motor repair

timing chain tensioner wear (HRA1 variant)
carbon buildup on intake valves

importantNotes: Cold-start rattles, overheating history, and oil use are the biggest warning signs before a petrol engine replacement becomes necessary.
Direct-injection petrol engines often need specialist attention for carbon build-up alongside the main mechanical repair.

items.title: Renault Electric Engines
items.description: Renault electric powertrains use high-voltage drive motors and supporting control systems rather than conventional engine internals. Failures are less common, but inverter faults, bearing noise, resolver issues, or battery-related drivetrain problems still require specialist diagnosis and replacement support.
items.descriptor: High-voltage drive units and EV systems
importantNotes: Electric drivetrain repairs should always include high-voltage safety checks and verification of the inverter and battery support systems.
Low mechanical wear does not eliminate replacement demand when control electronics or motor bearings begin to fail.
items.cta: Compare Renault electric engine prices

engineCodeDirectory.intro: Every major Renault engine code from the last two decades, grouped by family, with compatible models and typical reasons why owners seek replacement. Select your engine to compare rebuilt prices and get quotes from UK Renault specialists.

entries.title: K9K 830 1.5L Diesel
entries.description: Fitted to the Clio III, Megane II and Laguna II across UK markets (2005–2012). Replacement demand is primarily driven by injector tip coking, EGR valve seizure and premature turbo vane wear. Quality rebuilt units include Bosch-spec injectors, a revised EGR cooler, a balanced turbo cartridge and full compression verification before dispatch.
entries.cta: Get quotes for K9K 830 Renault engine replacement

entries.title: K9K 836 1.5L Diesel
entries.description: Powers the Captur I, Kadjar and Scenic III (2013–2020). Common failure points include high-pressure fuel pump wear, crankshaft position sensor faults and timing belt degradation from oil seal leaks. Reconditioned replacements feature an HPFP recalibration, reinforced timing cover, upgraded main bearings and a full gasket set.

entries.title: K9K 846 1.5L Diesel
entries.description: Used extensively in the Kangoo II and Trafic II vans (2010–2020). Owners typically seek replacement due to DPF blockage from short urban cycles, cylinder wall scoring from injector wash and head gasket stress. UK-supplied rebuilt engines come with a refreshed Euro-5 DPF, new cylinder head bolts, a reinforced oil pump and dyno testing.

entries.title: H5Ft 401 1.2L Turbo Petrol
entries.description: Found in early Clio IV and Captur I models (2012–2016). This unit is notorious for timing chain tensioner collapse, excessive piston ring oil consumption and turbo oil starvation. Our network’s rebuilt units feature an uprated timing chain kit, revised oil control rings, reinforced turbo feed lines and a carbon-cleaned intake manifold.
entries.cta: Get quotes for H5Ft 401 Renault engine replacement

entries.title: HRA1 420 1.2L Turbo Petrol
entries.description: Powers the Megane IV and Kadjar (2015–2019). Chain stretch leading to jumped timing, valve stem seal degradation and direct injection carbon buildup are the primary replacement drivers. Quality rebuilds include a heavy-duty chain tensioner, new valve guides, an updated PCV valve and full chemical decarbonisation.

entries.title: HRA1 430 1.2L Turbo Petrol
entries.description: Used in higher-spec Talisman and Espace V models (2015–2020). Common failures include turbo wastegate rattle, timing chain wear under sustained load and coolant ingress from cracked block grommets. Replacements are supplied with a billet turbo actuator, reinforced timing rails, a new water pump assembly and leak-tested passages.

entries.title: M9R 750 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Fitted to the Laguna III and Koleos I (2007–2015). Twin-turbo synchronisation failure, injector solenoid shorts and crankshaft main bearing wear drive most replacement enquiries. Upgraded rebuilds include a matched turbo pair, remanufactured injectors, uprated bearing shells and a fully balanced rotating assembly.
entries.cta: Get quotes for M9R 750 Renault engine replacement

entries.title: R9M 454 1.6L Diesel
entries.description: Powers the Megane IV, Kadjar and Trafic III (2014–2022). Timing chain guide fragmentation, oil pump drive shaft shear and EGR cooler internal leaks are frequent catastrophic points. Specialist rebuilds feature reinforced chain guides, a heavy-duty oil pump, an upgraded EGR matrix and pressure-tested cooling systems.

entries.title: M5M 988 1.6L Diesel
entries.description: Common in the Captur I and Clio IV (2013–2018). DPF pressure sensor faults, injector harness corrosion and camshaft lobe wear from poor oil maintenance trigger replacements. Reconditioned units include new camshaft followers, upgraded wiring looms, refreshed oil galleries and full ECU adaptation resets.

families.name: Legacy K4M / F4R / D4F Petrol Family (1998–2015)

entries.title: K4M 761 1.6L Petrol
entries.description: Widely used in Megane II, Scenic II and Laguna II (2002–2012). Ignition coil pack failure, variable valve timing solenoid blockage and water pump bearing collapse are typical failure modes. Replacements are supplied with upgraded coil packs, a revised VVT actuator, a high-flow water pump and a new tensioner set.
entries.cta: Get quotes for K4M 761 Renault engine replacement

entries.title: K4M 831 1.6L Petrol
entries.description: Fitted to Clio III, Modus and Logan (2005–2014). Owners commonly report rocker cover gasket oil leaks, thermostat housing cracking and piston slap at cold start. Quality rebuilds use silicone-sealed valve covers, aluminium thermostat housings, reinforced piston rings and verified compression testing.

entries.title: F4R 730 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: Found in Laguna II and Espace IV (2002–2010). VVT phaser rattle, coil-on-plug failure and oil consumption from valve stem seals drive demand. Upgraded units feature new VVT phasers, modern ignition coils, upgraded seal sets and full leak-down testing to factory specifications.

entries.title: F4Rt 750 2.0L Turbo Petrol
entries.description: The performance engine for Megane II/III RS (2004–2016). Turbo wastegate flutter, intercooler pipe splits and piston ring land cracking under high boost are known weaknesses. Specialist rebuilds include reinforced intercooler piping, upgraded turbo manifolds, forged piston replacements and high-flow oil pumps.
entries.cta: Get quotes for F4Rt 750 Renault engine replacement

entries.title: D4F 730 1.2L Petrol
entries.description: Powers Clio III and Twingo II (2005–2014). Coil pack short circuits, alternator belt tensioner failure and excessive carbon buildup in the intake are common. Rebuilt units feature high-output ignition coils, reinforced tensioner pulleys, walnut-shell intake cleaning and updated ECU mapping for reliability.

families.name: Commercial & Heavy Diesel Family (M9T / G9U / V9X)

entries.title: M9T 672 2.3L Diesel
entries.description: The workhorse engine for Master III and Trafic III (2010–2024). Dual-turbo actuator failure, injector copper washer leaks and timing chain stretch under heavy commercial loads are frequent. Replacements include upgraded twin-turbo sets, new copper sealing washers, heavy-duty timing kits and commercial-grade dyno runs.

entries.title: G9U 720 2.5L Diesel
entries.description: Used in Master II and Trafic II (2003–2014). Head gasket failure from thermal stress, crankshaft oil seal leaks and injector return line degradation are primary concerns. Quality rebuilds feature multi-layer steel head gaskets, viton crank seals, reinforced fuel return lines and pressure-tested cylinder heads.
entries.cta: Get quotes for G9U 720 Renault engine replacement

entries.title: V9X 726 3.0L V6 Diesel
entries.description: Fitted to Laguna III and Espace IV/V (2007–2015). Timing chain tensioner collapse, EGR valve carbon lock and turbocharger bearing failure drive most replacements. Upgraded units include uprated chain tensioners, refurbished EGR matrices, balanced turbo cores and a full vacuum system integrity check.

entries.title: F9Q 772 1.9L Diesel
entries.description: Powers Megane II, Scenic II and Laguna II (1999–2010). Injector nozzle drip, turbo boost solenoid failure and dual-mass flywheel rattle are common triggers. Rebuilt engines include refurbished injector nozzles, new electronic solenoids, lightweight solid flywheel conversions and a full sensor suite replacement.

entries.title: H4Jt 400 0.9L Turbo Petrol
entries.description: Used in Twingo III, Clio IV and Smart ForFour (2014–2022). Timing chain elongation, turbo oil feed clogging and piston ring wear from poor oil quality are key failures. Reconditioned units feature reinforced chain kits, upgraded turbo oil feed lines, low-friction piston rings and full compression testing.

engineYears.h2: Renault Engines by Year – What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: This section maps every major Renault engine change across the full UK model range - from the F-series petrol and F9Q diesel era of the late 1990s through the K9K dCi revolution, the 1.2 TCe and 1.3 TCe turbo petrol generations, the E-Tech hybrid family, and today's all-electric Zoe, Megane E-Tech and Scenic E-Tech platforms - so owners can identify their exact engine by vehicle year and compare rebuilt engine prices from UK specialists.

years.preview: F-Series Petrol, F9Q Diesel & Pre-K9K Era Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The late 1990s marked the final chapter of Renault's established F-series and E-series naturally aspirated petrols alongside the respected 1.9 dTi and 1.9 dCi F9Q diesel - a generation of engines that powered the Mégane Mk1, Laguna Mk1 and Clio Mk2, and whose replacement parts still move steadily through the UK aftermarket today.
keyChanges: F3R 2.0 16v petrol and F4R 2.0 16v continue from Mégane Mk1 through Laguna Mk1 as the volume petrol choice
E7J 1.4 and F3P 1.6 naturally aspirated petrols standard across Clio Mk2 and Mégane Mk1 entry variants
F9Q 1.9 dCi (diesel common-rail) introduced 1999 across Mégane and Laguna - Renault's first common-rail diesel unit
1.9 dTi (indirect injection turbodiesel) continues as the pre-common-rail diesel option in entry models
D7F / D4F 1.1/1.2 petrol introduced on Clio Mk2 (1998/2000) as the smallest petrol in the range
Euro 2 compliance governs early production; Euro 3 drives catalyst/EGR updates from 2001
mainEngines: E7J - 1.4 8v naturally aspirated petrol (Clio Mk2, Mégane Mk1)
F3P / K4M - 1.6 16v petrol (Clio Mk2, Mégane Mk1, Laguna Mk1)
F3R / F4R - 2.0 16v petrol (Mégane Mk1, Laguna Mk1, Scénic Mk1)
F9Q - 1.9 dCi - common-rail diesel (Mégane Mk1, Laguna Mk1, Kangoo)
D7F - 1.2 8v naturally aspirated petrol (Clio Mk2 early)
F8Q - 1.9 dTi - indirect turbodiesel (Mégane, Kangoo)
knownFor: K4M 1.6 16v a dependable naturally aspirated petrol across Clio and Mégane - timing belt adherence critical at 72,000-mile intervals; cam follower wear on neglected examples
F9Q 1.9 dCi earns a reasonable reliability reputation for its era; Delphi injector sensitivity to poor fuel quality the primary watch-point
F4R 2.0 16v widely regarded as one of Renault's most characterful petrol units - free-revving, responsive, used in Mégane Renaultsport; cam belt failure catastrophic if interval missed
1.9 dTi remains popular for simplicity and robustness pre-common-rail; limited performance by modern standards

years.preview: K9K 1.5 dCi Launch, Mégane Mk2 & Early Reliability Concerns Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The introduction of the K9K 1.5 dCi in 2001 - a product of the Renault-Nissan Alliance - was the defining engine event of this era: a compact common-rail diesel that would go on to become one of the most widely deployed diesel engines in automotive history, powering virtually every Renault and Dacia model for two decades, while its early incarnations earned a cautionary reliability reputation that the brand spent the following decade correcting.
keyChanges: K9K 1.5 dCi launched (2001) on Clio Mk2 and Mégane Mk2 - shared Renault-Nissan development; cast-iron block, 8v SOHC, timing belt
Mégane Mk2 (2002) establishes the K9K 1.5 dCi and K4M/K4J 1.6 petrol as its volume engines
2.0 dCi G9T introduced on Laguna Mk2 (2001) as the higher-output diesel option - twin-cam, 16v
Renault Vel Satis and Espace IV arrive with the 2.0 dCi and 3.0 V6 24v petrol options
Euro 3 firm; early K9K versions suffer significant connecting rod bearing failures due to extended oil change intervals
mainEngines: K9K - 1.5 dCi - 65/80/100hp - common-rail diesel (Clio Mk2, Mégane Mk2, Scénic Mk2, Kangoo)
K4M / K4J - 1.6 16v petrol (Mégane Mk2, Laguna Mk2)
F4R - 2.0 16v petrol (Mégane Mk2 Sport, Laguna Mk2)
G9T - 2.0 dCi - 150hp - diesel (Laguna Mk2, Vel Satis, Espace IV)
F9Q - 1.9 dCi - continued (Mégane Mk2, Laguna Mk2 entry)
V4Y - 3.0 V6 - naturally aspirated petrol (Vel Satis, Espace IV)
knownFor: Early K9K 1.5 dCi (pre-2007 builds) carries documented connecting rod bearing failure risk - caused by excessively long European oil service intervals of 20,000–30,000 miles; 6,000–9,000-mile oil changes mandatory for longevity
Delphi injector sensitivity in early K9K units - contaminated fuel accelerates injector seat wear from 70,000 miles
K4M 1.6 petrol in Mégane Mk2 is mechanically solid and represents the safest naturally aspirated buy; regular belt changes at 72,000 miles essential
G9T 2.0 dCi in Laguna and Vel Satis more powerful but more complex; high-mileage bearing wear mirrors K9K concerns - oil quality critical

years.preview: M9R 2.0 dCi, Scénic Mk3 & Revised K9K Major Engine Family Introduction Major Reliability Revision
years.description: The arrival of the M9R 2.0 dCi in 2006 - developed with Nissan - brought Renault a chain-driven, high-output diesel free from the bearing problems that plagued earlier units, while the K9K underwent its most significant internal revisions from 2007–2008, significantly improving long-term durability and establishing the 1.5 dCi as a genuinely reliable proposition.
keyChanges: M9R 2.0 dCi launched (2006) on restyled Laguna Mk2 and then Laguna Mk3 - timing chain-driven, 16v, 150/175hp variants; replaces G9T
K9K revised (2007–2008) with updated connecting rod bearing specification and shorter factory oil change intervals - a critical production correction
New Laguna Mk3 (2007) adopts M9R 2.0 dCi and updated K4M/K9K lineup
Mégane Mk3 (2008) launches with K9K revised 1.5 dCi, new M4R 2.0 16v CVT petrol, and F4R 2.0 Renaultsport
Koleos (2008) introduced as Renault's first UK SUV - powered by M9R 2.0 dCi and F4R 2.0 petrol
Euro 4 firm; DPF fitted as standard on M9R diesel variants
mainEngines: M9R - 2.0 dCi - 150/175hp - chain-driven diesel (Laguna Mk2/Mk3, Scénic Mk3, Koleos, Espace)
K9K (revised) - 1.5 dCi - 85/106hp - updated bearing spec (Mégane Mk3, Scénic Mk3, Clio Mk3)
K4M - 1.6 16v petrol (Mégane Mk3, Laguna Mk3)
F4R - 2.0 16v petrol (Mégane Renaultsport 225/230, Laguna Mk3)
D4F - 1.2 16v petrol continued (Clio Mk3, Modus)
knownFor: M9R 2.0 dCi is one of Renault's finest modern diesel achievements - chain-driven, high torque, capable of 300,000+ km with proper oil quality; main bearing wear can occur on severely oil-starved examples, but far rarer than K9K predecessors
Revised K9K from 2007–2008 onward is a transformed unit - specialists widely cite 2008+ as the safe buying threshold for 1.5 dCi used engines
F4R in Mégane Renaultsport carries genuine performance heritage - free-revving to 7,400rpm, characterful, well-regarded by enthusiasts; spark plug access awkward on later installations
Koleos 2.0 dCi M9R a capable unit in the SUV application; gearbox dual-mass flywheel wear more common than engine concerns

years.cta: Compare 2006–2010 Renault M9R and revised K9K replacement engine prices - Laguna, Mégane and Scénic specialists

years.preview: Zoe EV Debut, Energy Label & 1.2 TCe H5Ft Introduction Major Engine Family Introduction Technology Introduction
years.description: The debut of the Renault Zoe BEV (2013 UK) marked Renault's commitment to mainstream electric motoring ahead of most competitors, while the Energy 1.2 TCe turbocharged petrol introduced on Clio Mk4 (2012) replaced the ageing D4F naturally aspirated unit - and immediately acquired the timing chain reliability concern that would follow the H5Ft family throughout its production life.
keyChanges: Clio Mk4 (2012) launched with Energy H5F 1.2 TCe 120 turbocharged petrol and K9K 1.5 dCi diesel
H5Ft 1.2 TCe Energy (H5F) debuts - variable valve timing, direct injection, timing chain - replacing D4F naturally aspirated
Renault Zoe arrives in UK (March 2013) - first mainstream BEV from Renault; 22kWh battery, Q210 65kW motor, 130-mile NEDC range
K9K 1.5 dCi updated further for Euro 5 compliance - DPF standard, revised EGR, improved durability
R9M 1.6 dCi (130hp) debuts on Mégane Mk3 facelift and early Scénic - new Energy dCi family mid-range diesel replacing F9Q
Mégane Mk3 Renaultsport 265/275 Trophy uses highly developed F4R 2.0 16v with RS Monitor data - peak of naturally aspirated RS era
mainEngines: H5Ft (H5F) - 1.2 TCe Energy - turbocharged petrol 115/120hp (Clio Mk4, Mégane Mk3 late)
K9K (Euro 5) - 1.5 dCi - 75/90/110hp (Clio Mk4, Mégane Mk3, Scénic Mk3, Kangoo)
R9M - 1.6 dCi 130 - Energy diesel (Mégane Mk3, Scénic Mk3, Kadjar early)
M9R - 2.0 dCi - continued (Laguna Mk3, Espace, Koleos)
F4R - 2.0 16v RS - Renaultsport 265/275 (Mégane RS)
ZE Q210 motor - 65kW electric (Zoe 22kWh)

R9M 1.6 dCi 130 earns a strong reputation - piezoelectric injector system, good fuel economy, minimal documented failures; DPF management on short journeys the only routine concern
Renault Zoe Q210 proves the concept in UK use - 22kWh real-world range modest in cold weather (60–80 miles realistic) but pioneering for its price point
Mégane RS 265/275 with F4R celebrates the naturally aspirated RS era with forensic driver feedback and strong long-term reliability; highly collectible

years.preview: Kadjar, 1.3 TCe Alliance Engine & Zoe 41kWh Update Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The introduction of the Kadjar (2015) and the Mégane Mk4 (2016) brought Renault's partnership with Mercedes-Benz into the engine bay for the first time - the 1.3 TCe H5H is a jointly developed four-cylinder turbopetrol - while the Zoe's battery expanded to 41kWh, transforming real-world usability.

Mégane Mk4 (2016) debuts with 1.2 TCe and 1.6 dCi, previewing the 1.3 TCe H5H introduction in 2017
1.3 TCe H5H (130/140/160hp) - developed jointly with Mercedes-Benz - debuts from 2017 on Mégane Mk4 and Kadjar, replacing the H5Ft
Renault Zoe updated (2017) with new 41kWh Z.E. 40 battery and revised R110 electric motor - real-world range increases to 186 miles summer
K9K 1.5 dCi continues in Euro 6 specification with SCR AdBlue on higher-output versions
Scénic Mk4 (2016) launches as a crossover - 1.3 TCe and dCi engines inherited from Mégane platform
mainEngines: H5H - 1.3 TCe - 130/140/160hp - Mercedes co-developed turbopetrol (Mégane Mk4, Kadjar, Scénic Mk4)

K9K - 1.5 dCi Euro 6 - 90/110hp (Clio Mk4, Kadjar, Kangoo, Mégane Mk4)

R9M 1.6 dCi maintains its strong reliability record across Kadjar and Scénic applications - chain-driven, refined, DPF care on urban routes the principal concern
Zoe Z.E.40 41kWh transforms the model's usability - 186-mile summer range now credible for UK commuter use; battery health on earliest 22kWh examples can be variable
K9K Euro 6 with AdBlue SCR adds dosing unit as a new maintenance consideration; urban-cycle DPF management remains the primary owner concern

years.preview: Clio Mk5, Renewed Zoe 52kWh & E-Tech Hybrid Preview Technology Introduction
years.description: The Clio Mk5 (2019) signalled Renault's clearest step toward electrification in the mainstream segment - debuting the brand's CMF-B platform - while the Zoe received its most significant battery update yet (52kWh, 245-mile WLTP range) and Renault previewed the E-Tech hybrid system for imminent production.

years.preview: E-Tech Full Hybrid Launch, Arkana & Austral Era Major Engine Family Introduction Technology Introduction
years.description: The launch of the E-Tech full hybrid system in June 2020 on the Clio and Captur was Renault's most significant powertrain introduction since the K9K - a Formula 1-derived clutchless multi-modal hybrid using a 1.6-litre naturally aspirated petrol engine combined with two electric motors, delivering up to 80% electric driving in urban cycles without a plug.

faq.h2: Renault Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: ** Quick, honest answers to the most common questions Renault owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a Renault engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned Renault engine typically costs £1,200–£3,500 depending on the unit – K9K 1.5 dCi diesels sit at the lower end, while H5F 1.2 TCe petrols with timing chain upgrades command £2,000–£3,200. Supply-only prices exclude fitting, which adds £400–£900 labour depending on model complexity. Main dealers often quote £4,000–£6,500 for the same work, making independent specialists the preferred choice for cost-conscious owners.
items.cta: Compare Renault engine replacement prices now to see real-time quotes from UK specialists

items.question: What affects the cost of a Renault engine replacement?
items.answer: Four key factors drive pricing: engine displacement (1.2 TCe vs 2.0 dCi), condition tier (used vs reconditioned vs rebuilt), vehicle complexity (Clio vs Espace), and whether you choose supply-only or supply-and-fit. Location matters too – London and South East labour rates run 20–30% higher than regional specialists. Our comparison tool displays all these variables transparently so you can filter by budget and service type.
items.cta: Get quotes from UK Renault engine specialists to see how your specific model affects pricing

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my Renault?
items.answer: In most cases, yes – especially if the chassis, gearbox, and interior are in good condition. A rebuilt Renault engine with upgraded components (like reinforced timing chains on H5F units) often outlasts the original factory fitment. Scrapping a Megane or Captur worth £2,000–£4,000 rarely makes financial sense when a reconditioned engine costs £1,500–£3,000 fitted. If your Renault holds reasonable value, replacement is almost always the smarter investment.
items.cta: Use our free comparison tool to see whether a rebuilt or used Renault engine makes financial sense for your model

items.question: What's included in a Renault engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: Supply-and-fit covers sourcing your exact engine code (e.g., K9K, M9R), removal of the failed unit, installation of the replacement, fresh fluids and filters, a workmanship warranty, safe disposal of the old engine, and a post-fit test drive. Labour typically adds £400–£900 to the engine price, depending on accessibility – FWD hatchbacks are quicker than AWD Kadjars. Always confirm whether VAT, delivery, and ECU coding are included in the quoted price.
items.cta: Compare Renault engine supply and fit deals to find transparent, all-inclusive packages from vetted specialists

items.question: How long does a Renault engine replacement take?
items.answer: For common models like the Clio or Megane with readily available K9K or K4M engines, expect 3–5 working days from collection to completion. More complex vehicles (Espace, Laguna V6) or less common codes may require 5–7 days, with engine availability being the biggest variable. Our network prioritises high-demand codes like H5F 1.2 TCe and K9K 1.5 dCi to minimise downtime. When requesting quotes, ask suppliers about current lead times for your specific engine code to plan accordingly.
items.cta: Find reconditioned Renault engines with comprehensive warranty cover and confirmed availability

items.question: Do reconditioned Renault engines come with warranty?
items.answer: Yes – every reconditioned or rebuilt Renault engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty as standard. Many specialists offer extended 24-month coverage on premium rebuilt units, particularly for failure-prone families like the H5F 1.2 TCe. Warranty coverage typically includes major internals: crankshaft, pistons, timing components, oil pump, and cylinder head. Always confirm exact terms, exclusions, and claim procedures directly with your chosen supplier before committing.
items.cta: Find reconditioned Renault engines with comprehensive warranty cover to protect your investment long-term

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned Renault engine?
items.answer: Used engines cost less upfront (£400–£1,200) and suit temporary fixes or vehicles you plan to sell soon. Reconditioned units (£1,200–£3,500) include new bearings, seals, gaskets, and often upgraded components addressing known failures – like reinforced timing chains on H5F engines – plus a 12-month warranty and 100k+ mile potential. For failure-prone Renault families, we strongly recommend reconditioned with upgrades for long-term peace of mind.
items.cta: Compare used vs recon Renault engine options side-by-side to see which delivers better value for your ownership plans

items.answer: EngineMarket connects you with a UK-wide network of vetted Renault engine specialists, from Midlands diesel experts to London-based petrol rebuilders. While no single region dominates Renault specialist coverage, our registration lookup instantly matches your vehicle with nearby suppliers who stock your specific engine code and offer fitting. Nationwide delivery is available if no local stock exists, ensuring you're never limited by geography.
items.cta: Get quotes from UK Renault engine specialists to find the best combination of price, proximity, and warranty terms for your replacement

items.question: What are the most common Renault engine failures?
items.answer: The four most catastrophic failures driving replacement demand are: H5F 1.2 TCe timing chain stretch causing seizure at 50k–90k miles; K9K 1.5 dCi injector leakage and EGR carbon buildup; M9R 2.0 dCi injector hammer damaging pistons; and F9Q 1.9 dCi turbo bearing failure. These issues affect Clio, Megane, Captur, Laguna, and commercial vans across 2008–2020 models. Early detection rarely prevents escalation, making full replacement the most common and cost-effective outcome for affected owners.
items.cta: Compare Renault engine replacement prices now to address these known failure points with upgraded components

items.question: How can I get the best price on a Renault engine?
items.answer: Compare at least three quotes – prices for the same K9K or H5F engine can vary by £1,000+ across suppliers. Consider used units for tight budgets or short-term ownership, but opt for reconditioned with failure-point upgrades if you plan to keep the Renault long-term. Ask whether the quote includes fitting, delivery, VAT, and ECU coding to avoid hidden costs. Check supplier ratings and warranty terms before committing.
